Binary search trees are a fundamental data structure in computer science. They allow efficient searching, insertion, and deletion operations. In a BST, each node has at most two children. The left child is always less than the parent, and the right child is always greater. BSTs support O(log n) average-case search, insert, and delete.

L is T-recognizable if and only if some non-deterministic TM recognizes it. Adding non-determinism does not make the model more powerful! Proof sketch: Let A be T-recognizable Then there exists a deterministic TM D that recognizes it. Since any deterministic TM is also a non-deterministic TM, there exists a a non-deterministic TM that recognizes it. Let A be a language recognized by a non-deterministic TM M Simulate M using an equivalent deterministic TM D. Therefore there exists a deterministic TM that recognizes A. So A is T-recognizable. Enumerators Think about a TM with a printer that prints out strings after some computation. Set of all the strings printed b

erators Think about a TM with a printer that prints out strings after some computation. Set of all the strings printed by the enumerator E is the language of E. (Set of languages for which there exists an enumerator are known as recursively enumerable language) E may print out infinitely many strings. In which case L(E) has infinite cardinality. An enumerator generates a set of strings instead of recognizing. Similar to regex for RL, CFG for CFL. If w ∈ A, then M accepts w in say, ℓ steps. Therefore, E will print w in 𝑖 = ℓ-th iteration If w ∉ A, then M never accepts w. Therefore, E never prints it. Turing machines that output a string rather than accept/reject. L is T-recognizable if and only if some enumerator enumerates it. Proof sketch: Let A be T-recognizable. Let M be the TM that recognizes A. M be the TM that recognizes A. Construct an enumerator E that prints all (and only) the strings in A. E: on input w Ignore the input. For i = 1, 2, … Run M for i steps on each input {s1, s2, …, si} If M accepts sj, then print it. Set of first i strings in lexicographic order Enumerators L is T-recognizable if and only if some enumerator enumerates it. If w ∈ A, then E prints w. Therefore, M will accept w. If w ∉ A, then E never prints w. Therefore, M never accepts it. [Though it may run into infinite loop on certain inputs. Therefore, M is not a decider.] Think about a TM with a printer that prints out strings after some computation. Set of all the strings printed by the enumerator E is the language of E. Given a context free language A, show that A is decidable. i.e., there exists a TM that decides A. Note that since A is context free, it has a PDA P that accepts all strings in A and also rejects all strings that are not in A. Also, given P that recognizes A, we can simulate it using a TM ? Doesn’t work! Why? The PDA P might be non-deterministic. So, we can probably design a non -deterministic TM And then obtain an equivalent deterministic TM.
